name: nepeta cataria
intensity: Mild in typical tea-level use; stronger effects are uncommon.
mechanism: Likely mediated by volatile terpenes and other phytochemicals with mild calming and antispasmodic activity in preclinical data.
safetyNotes: Limited pregnancy/lactation safety evidence supports conservative use.
avoid in pregnancy due to uterine stimulation
Avoid combining with sedatives without clinical guidance.
carminative. Herbs often act via neurotransmitter modulation
nan.. Herbs often act via neurotransmitter modulation
description: A member of the mint family known for its euphoric effects on cats and mild sedative properties in humans. Traditionally used for colds, anxiety, and cramps.
preparation: Leaves and flowers dried for tea or tincture; calming and digestive herb (catnip)
region: Cultivated in Europe, North America, and other temperate regions.
sideEffects: avoid in pregnancy due to uterine stimulation
summary: Nepeta cataria is traditionally used for mild calming and digestive support, with limited modern clinical evidence.
